body.p:
Processing MethodsXSLT Streaming Consider the following problem. A comma-separated report is to be generated from this XML source.
UWE test footnote (23/40, callout W): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
With a for-each loop and a named template, this would work as follows:

note.p: Named templates, which are called directly via the Saxon invocation saxon -it:main, are useful when there is no single input source, e.g. because multiple sources need to be read, the input comes from a web service, or it is generated by the XSLT script itself.

body.p: This code can be simplified: Since we are reading from a file, we do not need a named template. Instead of the loop, we can also rely on the recursive descent of the XSLT processor:

If we want to process large data sets quickly - with a few hundred MB, it makes sense to switch to the new XSLT3.0 streaming option, because this avoids building an in-memory input tree. As already discussed in the chapter XSLT Accumulator, there are several ways to do this.
We will look at the xsl:iterator (docs) xsl:iterator construct and encounter some pitfalls along the way. First, the

li.p: We use xsl:source-document in conjunction with the streamable='yes' attribute to tell the processor that it should work in streaming mode.
If we read the source via a parameter, we must also start the transformation via a named template.

body.p: Without knowing exactly how XSLT streaming works, we tentatively place a series of value-of select statements in the iterator:
body.pre:
<xsl:template name="main"> <xsl:source-document href="{$input-file}" streamable='yes'> <xsl:iterate select="status-report/status-change"> <xsl:value-of select="concat(billing_id,',')"/> <xsl:value-of select="concat(claim_ids,',')"/> <xsl:value-of select="concat(status,',')"/> <xsl:value-of select="concat(format-dateTime(xs:dateTime(time_stamp), '[Y]-[M]-[D] [H]:[m]'),'
')"/> </xsl:iterate> </xsl:source-document> </xsl:template>
body.p: and are promptly rewarded with an error message:
body.pre: Static error on line 16 column 64 of report.xsl: XTSE3430: The body of the xsl:stream instruction is not streamable * There is more than one consuming operand: {xsl:value-of} on line 18, and {xsl:value-of} on line 19
body.p: So only one "consuming" value-of operation is allowed in this iterator. To select only once, we have to - at the expense of readability - restructure quite a bit. A solution could look like this, for example:
body.pre: <?xml version="1.0" ?> <xsl:stylesheet xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" exclude-result-prefixes="xs" xpath-default-namespace="https://tekturcms.de/schema/status-report/1.0" version="3.0"> <xsl:param name="input-file" required="yes"/> <xsl:output method="text"/> <!-- https://www.saxonica.com/html/documentation/xsl-elements/iterate.html --> <xsl:template name="main"> <xsl:source-document href="{$input-file}" streamable="yes"> <xsl:iterate select="status-report/status-change/*"> <xsl:choose> <xsl:when test="name()='time_stamp'"> <xsl:value-of select="concat(format-dateTime(xs:dateTime(time_stamp), '[Y]-[M]-[D] [H]:[m]'),' ')"/> </xsl:when> <xsl:otherwise> <xsl:value-of select="concat(.,',')"/> </xsl:otherwise> </xsl:choose> </xsl:iterate> </xsl:source-document> </xsl:template> </xsl:stylesheet>
body.p:
Here it is assumed that the element named 'time_stamp' appears last in the sequence, and upon its occurrence (
) a line break is inserted. The declarative approach from the first example is lost in this version.

note.p: Logically speaking, XSLT streaming programming is done at a lower level of abstraction in order to meet the requirements of the processor.

body.p: For a 1.6 GB file, the above script takes a good three minutes on my machine. The traditional template-match approach crashes with an out-of-memory exception, even when setting the Java heap size to 4GB.
